payload.summary: Reviewed the cited code paths and existing tests. Two amendments identify real robustness/correctness gaps: terminal runless replay can still match the wrong finalized attempt when backfilling a later run_id, and journal-repair merge logic can silently miss future fields. The allocation observation is non-blocking, and the proposed plan_hash replay backfill conflicts with explicit code comments and regression tests that intentionally avoid mislabeling legacy attempts after plan evolution.

votes.rationale: This is an optimization suggestion, not a correctness or safety defect. src/contexts/milestone_record/model.rs explicitly moved to canonical owned detail structs so parse and render share one representation and cannot drift. Reintroducing a borrowed or Cow-based render path would add API and lifetime complexity for a negligible cost on a non-hot path, with no demonstrated need.

In update_task_run, the run_id-specified path falls back from no exact match to unique_terminal_replay_match over all matching bead/project rows. That helper checks terminal status and run_id conflicts but does not check started_at for runless terminal candidates. As a result, a finalized legacy runless row with the wrong started_at can still be selected and have run_id backfilled onto it. That violates the started_at disambiguation rule already enforced for open runless matches, so this should be fixed and regression-tested.

The existing-row branches intentionally do not backfill plan_hash from the current snapshot, and the code comments plus regression tests in src/contexts/milestone_record/service.rs document why: persist_plan can advance snapshot.plan_hash after an attempt began, so stamping an older row from the current snapshot can mislabel history. Leaving a legacy row unlabeled is safer than assigning a potentially wrong plan_hash. This amendment assumes the snapshot hash is canonical for the original attempt, but the codebase explicitly treats that assumption as unsafe in replay and repair flows.

The shared StartJournalDetails and CompletionJournalDetails structs eliminate parse/render drift, but the repair merge logic in src/adapters/fs.rs still hand-enumerates the fields it compares and backfills. If a new field is added later, the code will still compile and the current round-trip tests can still pass, yet append_event_if_missing may silently omit that field during repair because merge ignores it. That is a real maintainability and data-integrity hazard in recovery code, so a merge-coverage test or a refactor that fails loudly on new fields is justified.
